verses.text: An Amalekite comes to David from the battle with torn clothes and dust on his head, reporting that Israel has fled and Saul and Jonathan are dead. David, who refused to harm Saul, now hears that his enemy and his dearest friend have fallen together. The news he dreaded has come.

An Amalekite comes to David from the battle with torn clothes and dust on his head, reporting that Israel has fled and Saul and Jonathan are dead. David, who refused to harm Saul, now hears that his enemy and his dearest friend have fallen together. The news he dreaded has come.

An Amalekite comes to David from the battle with torn clothes and dust on his head, reporting that Israel has fled and Saul and Jonathan are dead. David, who refused to harm Saul, now hears that his enemy and his dearest friend have fallen together. The news he dreaded has come.

An Amalekite comes to David from the battle with torn clothes and dust on his head, reporting that Israel has fled and Saul and Jonathan are dead. David, who refused to harm Saul, now hears that his enemy and his dearest friend have fallen together. The news he dreaded has come.

The Amalekite tells how he found Saul leaning on his spear, badly wounded, with the enemy closing in. He casts himself as the one present at the king's last moments — angling, perhaps, for reward. Those who bring news of the great often hope to profit by it.

The Amalekite tells how he found Saul leaning on his spear, badly wounded, with the enemy closing in. He casts himself as the one present at the king's last moments — angling, perhaps, for reward. Those who bring news of the great often hope to profit by it.

The Amalekite tells how he found Saul leaning on his spear, badly wounded, with the enemy closing in. He casts himself as the one present at the king's last moments — angling, perhaps, for reward. Those who bring news of the great often hope to profit by it.

He claims Saul begged him to finish the killing, and that he did so and took the crown to David. Whether true or a lie to win favour, he boasts of slaying the LORD's anointed. He badly misjudges David's heart, expecting thanks for the very thing David most abhors.

He claims Saul begged him to finish the killing, and that he did so and took the crown to David. Whether true or a lie to win favour, he boasts of slaying the LORD's anointed. He badly misjudges David's heart, expecting thanks for the very thing David most abhors.

He claims Saul begged him to finish the killing, and that he did so and took the crown to David. Whether true or a lie to win favour, he boasts of slaying the LORD's anointed. He badly misjudges David's heart, expecting thanks for the very thing David most abhors.

David and his men tear their clothes and weep and fast till evening for Saul, for Jonathan, and for the fallen of Israel. David does not gloat over a dead rival; he grieves. A noble heart mourns the fall even of its enemies, and feels the loss of God's people as its own.

David and his men tear their clothes and weep and fast till evening for Saul, for Jonathan, and for the fallen of Israel. David does not gloat over a dead rival; he grieves. A noble heart mourns the fall even of its enemies, and feels the loss of God's people as its own.

Learning the Amalekite confesses to killing the LORD's anointed, David has him executed: 'thy blood be upon thy head.' The man who thought murder would buy favour reaps death instead. David holds sacred what Saul forgot — that none may lift a hand against God's anointed and go free.

Learning the Amalekite confesses to killing the LORD's anointed, David has him executed: 'thy blood be upon thy head.' The man who thought murder would buy favour reaps death instead. David holds sacred what Saul forgot — that none may lift a hand against God's anointed and go free.

Learning the Amalekite confesses to killing the LORD's anointed, David has him executed: 'thy blood be upon thy head.' The man who thought murder would buy favour reaps death instead. David holds sacred what Saul forgot — that none may lift a hand against God's anointed and go free.

Learning the Amalekite confesses to killing the LORD's anointed, David has him executed: 'thy blood be upon thy head.' The man who thought murder would buy favour reaps death instead. David holds sacred what Saul forgot — that none may lift a hand against God's anointed and go free.

David composes a lament for Saul and Jonathan: 'how are the mighty fallen! Tell it not in Gath,' lest Israel's enemies rejoice. He grieves not for himself but for the honour of God's people. Even in sorrow, his concern is that the LORD's name not be mocked among the nations.

David composes a lament for Saul and Jonathan: 'how are the mighty fallen! Tell it not in Gath,' lest Israel's enemies rejoice. He grieves not for himself but for the honour of God's people. Even in sorrow, his concern is that the LORD's name not be mocked among the nations.

David composes a lament for Saul and Jonathan: 'how are the mighty fallen! Tell it not in Gath,' lest Israel's enemies rejoice. He grieves not for himself but for the honour of God's people. Even in sorrow, his concern is that the LORD's name not be mocked among the nations.

David composes a lament for Saul and Jonathan: 'how are the mighty fallen! Tell it not in Gath,' lest Israel's enemies rejoice. He grieves not for himself but for the honour of God's people. Even in sorrow, his concern is that the LORD's name not be mocked among the nations.

He calls the mountains of Gilboa to mourn, remembers Saul and Jonathan as 'lovely and pleasant in their lives,' and bids the daughters of Israel weep for the king who clothed them in scarlet. David honours all that was good in Saul, drawing a veil of grace over his faults.

He calls the mountains of Gilboa to mourn, remembers Saul and Jonathan as 'lovely and pleasant in their lives,' and bids the daughters of Israel weep for the king who clothed them in scarlet. David honours all that was good in Saul, drawing a veil of grace over his faults.

He calls the mountains of Gilboa to mourn, remembers Saul and Jonathan as 'lovely and pleasant in their lives,' and bids the daughters of Israel weep for the king who clothed them in scarlet. David honours all that was good in Saul, drawing a veil of grace over his faults.

He calls the mountains of Gilboa to mourn, remembers Saul and Jonathan as 'lovely and pleasant in their lives,' and bids the daughters of Israel weep for the king who clothed them in scarlet. David honours all that was good in Saul, drawing a veil of grace over his faults.

The lament closes with aching love for his friend: 'I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan... thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women.' 'How are the mighty fallen!' Grief like this, honest and tender, is no weakness but the mark of a great, loving heart.
